original_abstract: Selecting and validating robust classification and regression models remains a fundamental challenge in computational research. While cross-validation is the standard approach, many current state-of-the-art methodologies produce models characterized by high variance, which limits their utility in sensitive practical applications such as Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling. In this paper, we describe and evaluate a set of best practices designed to enhance model reliability and bolster confidence in the predictive performance of selected frameworks. A key operational component of the proposed workflow is the integration of rigorous error estimation and stability metrics, which ensure that the resulting models are both statistically sound and generalizable across diverse datasets.
humanized_abstract: Choosing and validating robust models for classification and regression remains a major issue in computational modeling. Although cross-validation is the most used methodology to choose models, new state of the art models often exhibit high variance, which is not desired when dealing with real-world application such as QSAR, which request robust models and high confidence in their performances. This work proposes and evaluates a set of good practices allowing to obtain robust models with high level of confidence. An error analysis and stability criterion are therefore considered as an operational component of the whole approach and to validate the robustness of the models and their reliability when dealing with different datasets.
